title: UVM Interview Question Bank
description: Interview preparation covering UVM architecture, factory, phasing, TLM, sequences, config_db, and methodology.

questions.prompt: What is the UVM factory and why is it important?
questions.rubric: Must explain that the factory decouples type creation from type identity, enabling type and instance overrides without modifying source code. Should mention type_id::create() vs new().
questions.model_answer: The UVM factory is a singleton registry that maps type names to constructors. Instead of calling new() directly, components call type_id::create(), allowing tests to substitute derived types via set_type_override_by_type() or set_inst_override_by_type() without modifying the original component code. This enables test-specific customization and reuse.

questions.prompt: How do objections work in UVM? What happens if you forget to drop one?
questions.rubric: Objections are cooperative counters that keep task-based phases alive. raise_objection increments, drop_objection decrements. Phase ends when count reaches zero. Forgetting to drop causes simulation hang.
questions.model_answer: Objections are reference counters on task-based phases (like run_phase). Each component calls phase.raise_objection() before work and phase.drop_objection() after. The phase ends only when the global count reaches zero. Forgetting to drop an objection causes an infinite hang. Use set_drain_time() as a safety net.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between uvm_config_db and uvm_resource_db?
questions.rubric: config_db is a convenience layer over resource_db with hierarchical path scoping. resource_db is global name/type lookup. config_db::set uses hierarchical context; resource_db::set uses a global scope string.
questions.model_answer: uvm_config_db is a typed, hierarchically-scoped configuration mechanism built on top of uvm_resource_db. config_db::set(context, inst_name, field_name, value) scopes the setting to a specific component subtree. resource_db::set(scope, name, value) uses a global string scope. config_db handles precedence: later sets at the same or higher hierarchy level override earlier ones. Always prefer config_db for component configuration.

questions.prompt: Explain the relationship between uvm_sequence, uvm_sequence_item, and uvm_sequencer.
questions.rubric: Sequence items are individual transactions. Sequences are generators that create and send items via start_item/finish_item. Sequencer arbitrates between sequences and provides items to the driver via seq_item_port.
questions.model_answer: uvm_sequence_item is a single transaction (data packet). uvm_sequence is a generator that creates sequence_items and sends them to a sequencer via start_item()/finish_item(). The uvm_sequencer arbitrates between multiple sequences and provides items to the driver through the seq_item_port/seq_item_export handshake. Sequences can be nested — a parent sequence can start child sequences.

questions.prompt: Does run_phase execute concurrently with the runtime sub-phases (reset_phase, main_phase, etc.)?
questions.rubric: YES — this is the senior trap. run_phase and the runtime sub-phases execute in parallel in separate domains. run_phase objections are independent of sub-phase objections. Both must complete for the run-time group to end.
questions.model_answer: Yes! run_phase and the runtime sub-phases (pre_reset through post_shutdown) execute concurrently in parallel domains. run_phase runs in the common domain while sub-phases run in the uvm_domain. Both sets of phases must complete (all objections dropped in both) before cleanup phases begin. A common trap: raising an objection only in run_phase but expecting it to keep main_phase alive — it won't, because they have independent objection counts. Similarly, if run_phase never drops its objection, the simulation hangs even if all sub-phases complete.

questions.prompt: What is an analysis port and how does it differ from a regular TLM port?
questions.rubric: Analysis port is one-to-many broadcast (write-only, non-blocking). Regular TLM port is one-to-one (put/get, can block). Analysis port calls write() on all connected subscribers.
questions.model_answer: uvm_analysis_port is a one-to-many broadcast port. When a monitor calls ap.write(txn), every connected subscriber receives a copy. It is non-blocking and cannot flow-control the sender. Regular TLM ports (uvm_blocking_put_port, uvm_blocking_get_port) are one-to-one and can block the caller. Use analysis ports for monitors broadcasting to scoreboards and coverage collectors.

questions.prompt: How do you coordinate stimulus across multiple protocol agents (e.g., AXI + APB) in a UVM environment?
questions.rubric: Use a virtual sequencer that holds handles to typed sub-sequencers. Virtual sequences run on the virtual sequencer and call child sequences on specific sub-sequencers. Must mention p_sequencer and casting.
questions.model_answer: Create a virtual_sequencer extending uvm_sequencer with handles to each protocol sequencer (axi_sqr, apb_sqr). In the env's connect_phase, assign these handles. Virtual sequences use `p_sequencer.axi_sqr` to target specific agents. The virtual sequence body() calls child sequences with child_seq.start(p_sequencer.axi_sqr). This coordinates cross-protocol traffic from a single control point.

questions.prompt: Design the UVM environment architecture for a DMA engine that bridges AXI master, APB slave config, and interrupt lines.
questions.rubric: Must include: AXI master agent, APB slave agent, interrupt agent, DMA register model (RAL), reference model/scoreboard, coverage model, virtual sequencer, and config objects. Should discuss scoreboard strategy for out-of-order DMA completion.
questions.model_answer: Environment contains: (1) AXI master UVC with driver/monitor/sequencer for DMA data path, (2) APB slave UVC for register configuration, (3) Interrupt agent for completion signaling, (4) RAL model for DMA registers with auto-prediction, (5) DMA reference model that predicts transfers from register writes, (6) Scoreboard with per-channel queues to handle out-of-order DMA completion, (7) Coverage: functional (channel configs, sizes, alignment), protocol (AXI burst types), and cross coverage, (8) Virtual sequencer coordinating config-then-transfer sequences. Key design decision: scoreboard uses channel ID as the ordering key since DMA channels complete independently.

questions.prompt: Your UVM simulation hangs at time 0 with no error messages. Walk through your debug methodology.
questions.rubric: Systematic approach: check objections (raise without drop), check for missing #delay in run_phase loops, check config_db failures, check factory registration, use +UVM_PHASE_TRACE, check for blocking TLM calls with no producer.
questions.model_answer: Debug steps: (1) Run with +UVM_PHASE_TRACE to see which phase is stuck. (2) Run with +UVM_OBJECTION_TRACE to see who raised without dropping. (3) If stuck in run_phase, check for infinite loops without time consumption (#delay or @event). (4) Check for blocking get() on an empty TLM FIFO (no monitor connected). (5) Check config_db — a failed get() with uvm_fatal may not appear if build_phase hasn't completed. (6) Check factory registration — missing `uvm_component_utils causes create() to return null. (7) Use simulator's thread debugger to see which process is blocked.

questions.prompt: How would you structure a UVM verification platform to support both simulation and emulation targets without maintaining separate environments?
questions.rubric: Must discuss: transaction-level acceleration (TLA) vs pin-level, abstract/concrete driver pattern, emulation-aware sequences, compile-time vs runtime configuration, and clock/reset abstraction.
questions.model_answer: Use a dual-mode architecture: (1) Abstract base driver with virtual drive_transaction() method — simulation subclass drives pin-level; emulation subclass uses TLA APIs. (2) Factory override selects the concrete driver based on a +UVM_TESTNAME or config_db setting. (3) Sequences remain unchanged — they generate transactions, not pin wiggles. (4) Monitors use a similar abstract/concrete pattern. (5) Remove $display and string formatting from synthesizable paths. (6) Use a clock generator abstraction that maps to real clock domains in simulation and to emulator-provided clocks in emulation. This lets 95% of the environment be shared.
